problem_summary: 2014 MCM Problem C (interdisciplinary "Network Science / measuring influence"). Given the Erdős-1 co-author dataset (511 researchers who collaborated directly with Paul Erdős), the team had to (1) build a co-author network, design influence measure(s), and rank the 511 researchers; (2) build a separate model to rank the influence/impact of research papers (and reason about ranking universities/departments/journals); (3) extend the models to a non-academic domain to test adaptability; and (4) discuss the validity and utility of the models. The team's thesis is that "influence" decomposes into a node's intrinsic *importance* in the network plus the *propagated* influence it absorbs from influential neighbors, and that one PageRank-style eigenvector machinery can serve all three sub-problems.
models_used: Graph/network model: researchers = nodes, co-authorship = undirected edges; G=(V,E), |V|=511. Same node-edge abstraction is reused for papers and for movie stars.

Node-degree analysis as a first (rejected-as-insufficient) importance proxy — k_i = sum of adjacency indicators; motivated by a connectivity-destruction argument (deleting the highest-degree node fragments the graph).
Clustering coefficient c_i = e_i / C(k,2) (Watts-Strogatz, ref [4]) to capture whether a node is a *bridge* between otherwise-disconnected neighbors (a degree-blind property).

Composite 'importance degree' p_i: combines an extended degree term f_i = k_i + sum of neighbor degrees with a normalized clustering term g_i (min-max normalized c_j/f_j), each passed through a 'chemotactic' L2-normalization u(x)=x/sqrt(sum x^2), then summed. (Adapted from Ren et al., ref [1].)

A hand-tuned piecewise function P_i(x) = 0.002·x^(1.1) for 0<x<65, capped at 0.2 for x>=65, encoding diminishing returns of the number of direct collaborations with Erdős; final importance q_i = P_i(x) + p_i.

PageRank as a dominant-eigenvector problem for 'influence degree': build importance-weighted adjacency A (a_ij = q_i), row-normalize to A-bar, transpose to transition matrix W=A-bar^T, solve WX = λX and take the non-negative eigenvector for λ_max as the influence scores. (Power-iteration / eigen-decomposition framing.)
Directed citation network for papers (15 papers): edges point along citation; same PageRank eigenvector machinery yields a 'cited index' r per paper.

AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process) for paper influence: 3x3 pairwise-comparison matrix over {Impact Factor, H-index, cited-index r} with imposed ordering IF > r > H, solved in YAAHP to weights α_IF=0.5469, α_H=0.1085, α_r=0.3445; final score T_i = α_IF·IF_i + α_H·H_i + α_r·r_i. Consistency checked (CI=0.0516, CR=0.02 < 0.1).

Model extension to Chinese movie stars: replace Erdős with Tony Leung as the hub, co-starring = edges (31-32 actors), normalized 5-year average box-office as the 'importance' input, PageRank for influence ranking — used as an external validity check against real-world fame.

Sensitivity/error analysis via a Deviation Degree metric E_i = (1/n)·sum_j |o_ij - m_ij| (mean rank shift) for node deletion in the co-author network, and E as a function of the citation feedback ratio u in the citation network.
key_techniques: Clean conceptual decomposition into two named, defined quantities — 'importance degree' (intrinsic network position) vs 'influence degree' (importance propagated through neighbors) — and holding that distinction consistently through every model and the results discussion.

Motivating each modeling choice with a concrete counterexample on a 10-node toy graph (Figures 1-4): showing that two nodes of equal degree differ in bridging value justifies adding the clustering coefficient — a textbook 'why the simple metric fails' narrative.

Reusing one engine (importance-weighted PageRank eigenvector) across three distinct objects — researchers, papers, actors — which is exactly what Problem C rewards (a transferable methodology, not three bespoke models).

Separating a paper's influence into 'external' (citation-propagated r via PageRank) and 'internal' (author H-index + journal IF) drivers, then fusing them with AHP — a hybrid network + multi-criteria-decision design.
Using AHP consistency tests (CI, CR) as a built-in self-audit of the subjective weights, pre-empting the obvious 'your weights are arbitrary' critique.

Validating an unfalsifiable construct (academic 'influence') indirectly: porting the model to movie stars where ground-truth fame is common knowledge, then arguing the ranking 'maintained high consistency with reality.'

Quantitative sensitivity analysis with a custom mean-absolute-rank-shift metric E, deleting low/medium/high-importance nodes and sweeping the citation feedback parameter u to find a stable operating interval [0.7, 1].

Mining the rank deltas (p-rank minus q-rank) to surface interpretable 'stable / positive / negative' cohorts (Table 3) and turn raw numbers into an actionable insight: a low-importance 'young researcher' can boost influence by co-authoring with high-importance partners.
why_it_won: "From a judge's seat, this paper wins on the three things Problem C actually grades. (1) Methodology fit: 'measuring influence' is a PageRank-shaped problem, and the team recognized that and built importance-weighted eigenvector centrality rather than ad-hoc scoring — and crucially proved its generality by re-running the identical engine on papers and on movie stars. That directly answers tasks 3 and 4 ('examine adaptability', 'discuss science and utility') with evidence, not assertion. (2) Modeling judgment shown openly: rather than jumping to the answer, it walks degree → clustering coefficient → composite, each step justified by a toy-graph counterexample, so the reader trusts that the final metric exists for a reason. (3) Insight beyond ranking: the importance-vs-influence distinction plus the Table-3 cohort analysis produces a genuine, quotable finding (collaborate with high-importance partners to raise your own influence) — judges reward a model that *explains*, not just sorts. The movie-star extension is the clincher: validating an intangible against a domain everyone can sanity-check is a sophisticated move that signals the team understood what 'utility' means. Combined with the visible self-audit (AHP CI/CR, the E-based sensitivity sweeps), the work reads as careful, transferable, and honest — the Outstanding profile."
section_highlights: "Structure is conventional but disciplined and complete: Summary that states each model and its headline result (ALON NOGA M = most influential researcher; 'Statistical mechanics of complex networks' = most influential paper); Assumptions; Symbol table; then one section per object (researchers, papers, extension) each ending in result analysis; Sensitivity; Strengths/Weaknesses. The strongest writing moves: (a) defining 'importance degree' and 'influence degree' up front and never conflating them; (b) the Figure 1-4 deletion-of-a-node visual argument that makes an abstract centrality idea tangible; (c) Table 3's color-coded stable/positive/negative cohorts, which converts a rank-comparison table into a narrative with a takeaway; (d) the six-degrees-of-separation hook in the intro that frames why networks matter. Weaknesses in presentation: heavy ESL/typographic noise (broken 'Erdős' rendering, equation (4.2.2) cross-ref, a duplicated movie-star paragraph, CR formula mistyped as CI/CR), and figures that are screenshots of small toy graphs rather than a rendering of the actual 511-node network. The math is communicated through equations + one worked toy example each, which keeps it readable."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Tiny, hand-picked evidence base for the paper model: only 15 papers (self-admitted), so the citation PageRank is computed on a toy graph and the 'most influential paper' claim is weakly supported. The extension uses only ~31-32 actors.

Several magic numbers with no derivation or sensitivity check: P_i(x)=0.002·x^1.1 capped at 0.2, the 65-collaboration cutoff, and the AHP pairwise entries (1,4,2 / ...) are asserted, not justified; AHP weights are inherently subjective (acknowledged in Weaknesses).

Adjacency-matrix definition is stated backwards/confusingly: 'a_ij = 0 if i and j connect, q_i if they don't connect' (Eqs. 9 and 14) is almost certainly the opposite of intent and is never reconciled — a correctness red flag a sharp judge would catch.

The PageRank framing is non-standard: it solves WX=λX for the top eigenvector but omits the damping factor / teleportation and never discusses dangling nodes, convergence, or why the largest eigenvalue's eigenvector is the right object here.

Result instability is visible but under-addressed: the sensitivity sweep of u (Figure 7) is wildly non-monotonic (E jumps 0.1 → 2.8 between adjacent u values), which undercuts the 'our model is stable' claim; they rescue it by declaring [0.7,1] the good interval rather than explaining the volatility.

Validation is qualitative ('highly consistent with reality') with no quantitative agreement metric (e.g., rank correlation against a real fame/box-office ranking), so the movie-star 'proof' is suggestive, not rigorous.

Erdős himself is excluded (per the prompt) but the entire importance signal still leans on 'times of cooperation with Erdős', making the model Erdős-centric and not obviously generalizable to networks lacking a single dominant hub — a limitation the actors extension actually exposes (they note picking Tony Leung as sole core is 'not practicable').
Mixing IF, H-index, and r (different units/scales) in T_i = α_IF·IF + α_H·H + α_r·r without normalizing them to a common scale makes the weighted sum dimensionally inconsistent.
reusable_patterns: The 'importance vs influence' split: define one intrinsic node property and one propagated property, compute the intrinsic one first, then feed it as edge/teleport weights into PageRank to get the propagated one. A clean, reusable template for any 'who matters in this network' prompt.

Importance-weighted PageRank recipe: build adjacency from a per-node weight, row-normalize, transpose to a column-stochastic transition matrix, take the dominant eigenvector. Generic centrality engine portable across domains.

Network + AHP hybrid for multi-factor scoring: use a network/PageRank metric for the 'propagated' factor and AHP (with CI/CR consistency check) to fuse it with non-network covariates (here author quality + venue quality). Good pattern whenever influence has both relational and attribute drivers.

Counterexample-driven metric escalation: introduce the naive metric (degree), break it with a small toy graph, then add the fix (clustering coefficient) — a persuasive way to justify model complexity to judges instead of asserting it.

Cross-domain validation of an intangible: re-instantiate the same model in a domain with observable ground truth (celebrity fame, box-office) to argue external validity for a construct you can't directly measure. Directly satisfies Problem C's 'test adaptability / discuss utility' tasks.

Deletion-based sensitivity with a mean-rank-shift metric E_i = (1/n)Σ|o_ij − m_ij|: perturb the network (remove high/med/low nodes, sweep a key parameter) and report how much the ranking moves — a concrete, presentable robustness check for any ranking model.

Rank-delta cohorting: compute the change between two rankings, bucket nodes by the sign/magnitude of the change, and read off an actionable behavioral insight (e.g., partner with high-importance nodes to climb). Turns a results table into a story.

Diminishing-returns saturation via a capped piecewise function for any 'more of X helps, but only up to a point' feature — a simple, defensible feature-engineering move (just remember to sensitivity-test the breakpoint).
